plainMeaning: This verse counts the eternal Jina-temples in the gods' realms — countless ones among the wandering and luminary gods, and exactly 84,97,023 (eighty-four lakh, ninety-seven thousand and twenty-three) in the highest heavenly palaces.
recitation: vaṇaceiya saṅkhaguṇo, joisiesu tahā vimāṇesu. tevīsāhiya sahasā, sagaṇaui lakkha culasīi.

wordByWord.etymology: vaṇa (< Skt vāṇa-vyantara, the 'peripatetic' deities) + ceiya (< Skt caitya, 'shrine'); i.e. the shrines of the vyantara gods
wordByWord.notes: Resumes the vyantara realm whose shrines the previous verse called asaṅkhya (innumerable).

wordByWord.etymology: saṅkha (< Skt saṅkhyā, 'number') + guṇa ('-fold, times'); 'a number-fold', hence very numerous
wordByWord.notes: Read here as 'manifold/beyond count'; some editions parse asaṅkha-guṇa, 'innumerable-fold', consonant with the realm's traditional asaṅkhyāta tally.

wordByWord.etymology: < Skt jyotiṣka, the luminary deities — sun, moon, planets, constellations and scattered stars; locative plural

wordByWord.notes: Carries the sense 'manifold/innumerable' over from the vyantara clause to the jyotiṣka and vaimānika realms.

wordByWord.gloss: in the vimānas (celestial palaces of the vaimānika gods)
wordByWord.etymology: < Skt vimāna, 'aerial palace, heavenly mansion'; locative plural — by extension the vaimānika deva-class
wordByWord.notes: The precise count that follows is the vaimānika śāśvata-caitya tally.

wordByWord.etymology: tevīsa (< Skt trayoviṁśati, '23') + ahiya (< Skt adhika, 'more, in excess')

literalTranslation: The vyantara shrines [are] manifold; likewise in the jyotiṣka [heavens]; and in the vimānas — [the eternal Jina-temples numbering] eighty-four lakh, ninety-seven thousand, increased by twenty-three (84,97,023).
idiomaticTranslation: The shrines among the wandering vyantara gods are beyond reckoning, and so too in the realms of the luminary jyotiṣka gods; while in the soaring vimānas of the heavenly vaimānika gods the eternal Jina-temples stand eighty-four lakh, ninety-seven thousand and twenty-three in number.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse continues, without pause, the cosmic census begun just before it — where seven crore seventy-two lakh shrines were counted in the bhavanapati mansions and 'innumerable' ones in the vyantara cities. Here the count sweeps upward through the remaining two of the four orders of gods. The vyantara shrines are again called 'manifold' (saṅkhaguṇo), the jyotiṣka realm 'likewise' (tahā) beyond fixed tally — both classes whose shrines the cosmographies leave uncounted as asaṅkhyāta. Then comes the verse's deliberate hinge: against these uncountable realms the poet sets one exact, famous figure for the vaimānika heavens — 84,97,023. The shift from the vague 'manifold' to a precise, memorisable number is the point. Having let the reciter bow to shrines past counting, the hymn now hands them a definite total they can hold in mind, the canonical vaimānika tally preserved in the cosmographic literature. Added to the bhavanapati shrines and the small fixed count of the human world, it yields the traditional grand total of eternal caityas. The ordering — vyantara, jyotiṣka, vimāna — also traces the standard upward ladder of the deva-nikāyas, so that the single sentence carries the worshipper from the mid-world peripatetic gods up to the highest empyrean. The whole couplet is thus one act of homage rendered as arithmetic: every figure is a multitude of shrines the reciter venerates at a stroke.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the doctrine of śāśvata (beginningless, uncreated) Jina-images permanently enshrined throughout the cosmos, distributed across the four orders of gods — bhavanapati, vyantara, jyotiṣka and vaimānika. Vyantara and jyotiṣka shrines are doctrinally asaṅkhyāta (innumerable), which is why the verse gives them no fixed sum; the vaimānika heavens, by contrast, carry the precise eternal-temple count 84,97,023. The teaching is that Jina-veneration is built into the very fabric of the universe, independent of any human founder or historical date — worship that the worshipper's own bow now joins.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse lets the worshipper, without leaving the prayer hall, bow at once to every temple in the gods' heavens — the countless shrines of the wandering and star gods and the 84,97,023 in the celestial palaces. Dwelling on a number so vast cultivates cosmic humility and reinforces that reverence for the Jina is eternal and universal, not a merely local or recent act.

crossReferences.text: Bṛhat-saṅgrahaṇī (gāthās enumerating śāśvata caityas and bimbas across the four deva-nikāyas)
Tattvārtha-sūtra, ch. 4 (the four classes of devas: bhavanapati, vyantara, jyotiṣka, vaimānika)
Sakala-tīrtha vandanā enumeration of śāśvata Jina-caityas in the three worlds

sources.title: Jain cosmology — the four orders of gods (bhavanapati, vyantara, jyotiṣka, vaimānika) and the structure of the three worlds
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the four deva-nikāyas and the upper-world structure in which the eternal shrines are enumerated

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Institute of Indology and Tamil Studies, Cologne University)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots for the numerals and nouns (trayoviṁśati, saptanavati, caturaśīti, lakṣa, sahasra, vimāna, caitya, tathā)

sources.title: Anchalgacchna Aetihasik Lekho (book summary), JainGPT
sources.consultedFor: Anchalgachchha tradition context for the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā and its śāśvata-shrine enumeration

notes: The figure 84,97,023 (84 lakh, 97 thousand, 23) is the well-attested count of vaimānika śāśvata caityas in the Jain cosmographic tradition (Bṛhat-saṅgrahaṇī and related works); it is read here as governing 'vimāṇesu'. The vyantara and jyotiṣka realms are given no fixed total, matching their traditional classification as asaṅkhyāta (innumerable). 'saṅkhaguṇo' is taken as 'manifold/many-fold'; an alternative parse as 'asaṅkha-guṇa' ('innumerable-fold') is plausible and would align even more closely with the previous verse's 'asaṅkhe' for the vyantara shrines — flagged as a minor reading ambiguity. Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa orthography of the source (e.g. ચેઇય for caitya, સગનઉઇ for saptanavati) is preserved verbatim and not normalised.
